cards.back: A method of accounting that recognises revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of when cash is received or paid.
A method of accounting that recognises revenue only when cash is received and expenses only when cash is paid.
Revenue is recognised when it is earned (service performed or goods delivered), not when cash is received.
Expenses must be matched to the period in which they help generate revenue, ensuring profit is fairly calculated.
Cash paid before an expense is incurred. It is recorded as an asset (e.g., Prepaid Insurance) and expensed gradually as the benefit is consumed.
An expense incurred but not yet paid. It is recorded as a liability (e.g., Salaries Payable) to match the expense to the period incurred.
Revenue earned but not yet received in cash. It is recorded as an asset (Accounts Receivable) to recognise the revenue in the correct period.
Cash received before a service is performed or goods delivered. It is recorded as a liability (deferred revenue) until the service is completed.

cards.front: How do you record a prepaid expense payment?
cards.back: Dr Prepaid [Expense] [Amount] / Cr Cash [Amount]. Later, as the benefit is consumed, adjust with Dr [Expense] Cr Prepaid [Expense].

cards.front: How do you record an accrued expense?
cards.back: Dr [Expense] [Amount] / Cr [Payable] [Amount]. This matches the expense to the period incurred.

cards.front: What account is used for accrued expenses?
cards.back: A payable account (liability) such as Salaries Payable, Interest Payable, or Utilities Payable. It represents the obligation to pay.

cards.front: What is the relationship between unearned revenue and the income statement?
cards.back: Unearned revenue is a liability on the balance sheet. As the service is performed, it transfers to revenue on the income statement.
